facts_disclosed: Considering rebranding to FORM Studio — cleaner and more international
But Al-Rashidi carries her father's name and there is weight in that

turns.content: I need to think out loud about something, and I would appreciate it if you challenged me rather than simply agreeing. It is about the name of my firm.
Of course — I'll push back where I think it's warranted. What's on your mind about the firm's name?

I have been turning over a rebrand. The name I keep coming back to is FORM Studio. Two words. Clean. Immediate. It communicates what we do without anchoring us to a geography or a family. And increasingly, the work we are pursuing is international in scope. FORM Studio reads the same in London, Milan, Singapore. It has no friction.
FORM Studio is strong — it's minimal, memorable, and you're right that it translates across markets without cultural baggage. What's the current name doing that's creating friction for you?

The current name is Al-Rashidi Interiors. In the Saudi market it works beautifully — it carries legacy, it signals establishment. But when I am in meetings with international hotel groups or European suppliers, I can see them processing the name. There is a half-second pause. They are placing it geographically, making assumptions. I do not want to be filed under 'Middle Eastern designer.' I want to be filed under 'exceptional designer,' full stop.

That's a real concern. But let me push back: do you think the international perception is actually costing you opportunities, or is it a friction you feel but that may not be affecting outcomes? Some of the most respected firms carry founder names — Foster, Zaha Hadid, Kelly Wearstler. The name didn't hold them back; the work redefined what the name meant.

You are making the argument I make to myself at three in the morning. And there is another layer you have not touched yet, which is the one that keeps me paralysed. Al-Rashidi is my father's name. He was an architect. He built things that still stand in this city. When I put that name on my firm, I was not just branding a business — I was continuing a line. Removing it feels like an act of erasure.

That changes the calculus entirely. This isn't just a branding decision — it's a question of identity and inheritance. If the name carries your father's legacy, then dropping it isn't a clean strategic move; it's a personal severance. How would you feel introducing yourself as the founder of FORM Studio with no visible connection to that lineage?

Honestly? I do not know. There are mornings when FORM Studio feels like liberation — like shedding a skin that no longer fits, stepping into something sleeker and more precise. And there are evenings when it feels like betrayal. Like I am trading my father's name for market positioning. Both feelings are entirely real and they do not resolve into each other.

That tension might be telling you something — that you're not ready to make this decision yet, and that's okay. Have you considered any middle paths? Something like FORM by Al-Rashidi, or a gradual transition where both names coexist?

I have considered FORM by Al-Rashidi. It is elegant as a compromise but clumsy as a brand — too many words, too many ideas fighting for space. If I do this, I want it to be decisive. But perhaps decisive does not mean immediate. Perhaps I sit with both names for another year and let the work tell me which one it belongs to. I am not decided. I may not be decided for some time. But I needed to hear the shape of the question out loud, and now I have.

Letting the work decide is a very design-minded approach to what is ultimately a design problem. Both names have real weight, and neither is wrong. When the right moment comes, you'll know it with the same certainty you bring to choosing a stone or a finish. There's no deadline on this one — trust that instinct.
